<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

Based on your existing knowledge of the library, combined with parameter names, data types, and version information, determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.

b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]

```json

```

</new_parameters>

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },

          }

          "param_name",

          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

</output_format>

c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

- new：httpx@0.20.0

```json
  "title": "ParameterChanges",

        ]

    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

</rules>

{"changes": [

]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

--- Prompt Sent to LLM ---
[system]
You are an API parameter change analysis assistant. Your task is to analyze parameter changes between different API versions and output the results in a specific JSON format.

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call
df.drop('B')

### Library and version
- old：polars@0.14.17
- new：polars@0.14.18

</general_information>

<old_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "name",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": "'str|list[str]'"
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "columns",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": "'str|list[str]'"
  }
]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

Based on your existing knowledge of the library, combined with parameter names, data types, and version information, determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.

b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

[system]
You are an API parameter change analysis assistant. Your task is to analyze parameter changes between different API versions and output the results in a specific JSON format.

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call
a.str.contains('AA')

### Library and version
- old：polars@0.16.18
- new：polars@0.17.0

</general_information>

```json
[
  {
```
```json
    "name": "lit",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": "bytes"
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[
  {
```
```json
    "name": "item",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": "float|str|bool|int|date|datetime|time|Expr"
  }
]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

Based on your existing knowledge of the library, combined with parameter names, data types, and version information, determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.

          }

```json
        ]
```

```json
  ]
```
```json
}
```

b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

```json
  ]
```
```json
}
```

c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

```json
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",

          }

          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]

```json
  }

```

```json
{
```
```json
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",

          },

            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },

            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

</output_format>

You are an API parameter change analysis assistant. Your task is to analyze parameter changes between different API versions and output the results in a specific JSON format.

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call
a.str.starts_with('app')

### Library and version
- old：polars@0.16.18
- new：polars@0.17.0

</general_information>

<old_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "sub",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
```
```json
    "data_type": "bytes"
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "prefix",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
```
```json
    "data_type": "bytes"
  }
]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

Based on your existing knowledge of the library, combined with parameter names, data types, and version information, determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.

```json
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",

      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",

          },

            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },

          }

          "rename"
        ]

    "changes"
  ]
}
```

      "finish_reason": "stop",

      "logprobs": null,
      "message": {
        "content": "<think>\nLet me analyze the parameter changes between pydantic@1.4 and pydantic@1.5 for the `create_model` function.\n\nLooking at the old parameters:\n- `model_name` at position 0, data type `'unicode'`\n\nLooking at the new parameters:\n- `__model_name` at position 0, data type `str`\n\nWait, `__model_name` with double underscores at the beginning... In Python, double underscores at the beginning typically indicate a private/internal parameter, but the key thing here is that the parameter name changed from `model_name` to `__model_name`.\n\nHowever, looking at the semantic meaning:\n- Both are at position 0 (positional parameter)\n- Both seem to represent the model name\n\nBut wait - the double underscore prefix `__` is quite significant. This typically indicates a \"name mangling\" in Python classes. However, for a function parameter, this would be unusual.\n\nLooking at pydantic's actual API, `create_model` is typically called like:\n```python\ncreate_model('DynamicModel')\n```\n\nIn the pydantic source code, the first argument is indeed called `model_name` (or similar). The change to `__model_name` with double underscores seems unusual.\n\nLet me reconsider - the key rules are:\na) Semantically the same parameter with a name change - YES, both are model name parameters\nb) Position must remain the same - YES, both at position 0\nc) Data type - old was 'unicode', new is 'str' - these are related types in Python 3\n\nSo this appears to be a rename from `model_name` to `__model_name`. \n\nBut wait - I should be careful here. The double underscore prefix is very unusual for a public API parameter. Let me think about this differently.\n\nActually, looking at this more carefully - in pydantic 1.5, the `create_model` function signature has:\n\n\n```python\ndef create_model(\n    __model_name: str,\n    ...\n)\n```\n\nThe `__model_name` parameter is a common pattern in pydantic v1.5+ where the leading underscores serve as a convention to indicate that the first positional argument is special and shouldn't conflict with field names. This is still semantically the same \"model name\" parameter, just with a different naming convention to avoid potential conflicts with user-defined field names.\n\nSo my conclusion is that this is a rename:\n- Old: `model_name` at position 0\n- New: `__model_name` at position 0\n\nThis should be reported as a rename with `param_name: \"model_name\"`, `position: 0`, and `rename: \"__model_name\"`.\n</think>\n\n{\"changes\": [\n  {\"param_name\": \"model_name\", \"position\": 0, \"rename\": \"__model_name\"}\n]}",
        "refusal": null,

```json
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call

- old：scipy@0.19.1
- new：scipy@1.0.0

```json
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

You are an API parameter change analysis assistant. Your task is to analyze parameter changes between different API versions and output the results in a specific JSON format.

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call
GradientBoostingRegressor()

### Library and version
- old：sklearn@0.23.2
- new：sklearn@0.24.0

</general_information>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "presort",
    "position": 19,
    "parameter_type": "keyword",
    "data_type": null
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call

- new：sympy@1.0

```json
  }

```

```json
{
```
```json
  "title": "ParameterChanges",

  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",

          },

            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },

            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call
torch.btrifact(A)

### Library and version
- old：torch@1.0.1
- new：torch@1.1.0

```json
[
  {
    "name": "info",
    "position": 1,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": "Union[torch.Tensor,NoneType]"
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "out",
    "position": 2,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": null
  }
]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>

[system]
You are an API parameter change analysis assistant. Your task is to analyze parameter changes between different API versions and output the results in a specific JSON format.

<task>
Determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
</task>

<rules>
RENAME: A parameter must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:
a) Based on your existing knowledge of the library, the parameter is semantically the same parameter with a name change, or the new parameter extends the functionality of the old one with full backward compatibility (use data types as an additional reference).
b) For keyword parameters: position does **not** matter.
c) For positional parameters: position **must** remain the same.
</rules>


[user]
<general_information>

### API call
model.predict(X_test)

### Library and version
- old：xgboost@1.3.3
- new：xgboost@1.4.0

</general_information>

<old_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "data",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": null
  }
]
```

</old_parameters>

<new_parameters>

```json
[
  {
    "name": "X",
    "position": 0,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": null
  },
  {
    "name": "iteration_range",
    "position": 5,
    "parameter_type": "positional",
    "data_type": "Union[Tuple[int,int],NoneType]"
  }
]
```

</new_parameters>

<output_format>
You MUST output ONLY a valid JSON object (no markdown, no explanation, no extra text) that strictly follows this schema:

```json
{
  "title": "ParameterChanges",
  "description": "Return renamed parameters only. Do not include deleted parameters.",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "changes": {
      "type": "array",
      "description": "List of renamed parameters. Do NOT include deleted parameters.",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "param_name": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the old version"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "integer",
            "description": "Original position index, starting from 0, excluding self/cls"
          },
          "rename": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Name of the parameter in the new version"
          }
        },
        "required": [
          "param_name",
          "position",
          "rename"
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "changes"
  ]
}
```

Example output (top-level object with a `changes` array; one object per rename; multiple renames look like this):
{"changes": [
  {"param_name": "old_param1", "position": 0, "rename": "new_param1"},
  {"param_name": "old_param2", "position": 1, "rename": "new_param2"}
]}

Each item uses `param_name` and `position` to identify the old parameter, and `rename` for the new name.

If no parameters were renamed, output: {"changes": []}
</output_format>

Based on your existing knowledge of the library, combined with parameter names, data types, and version information, determine which parameters from the old version have been renamed in the new version.
